27Module 1 Summary
Legal Framework

Health and Safety at Work Act 2015 (HSWA)

HSWA is NZ's main workplace health and safety law which aims to:

  • Protect workers and other people from harm
  • Make sure risks are managed
  • Make businesses and workers accountable for their responsibilities
HSWA Purpose
"Workers and other persons should be given the highest level of protection against harm to their health, safety, and welfare..."
Key Hazard

Most of Our Hazardous Substances Are Flammable

Bunnings store, Perth, 2018

Most of the hazardous substances that we sell are flammable (can catch fire).

Just like at Bunnings store in Perth in 2018.

📋 Bunnings Perth Fire (2018)
  • A fire started in the shop.
  • Flammable products inside intensified the fire.
  • Explosions occurred due to LPG cylinders and aerosols.
  • The building burnt to the ground in less than 15 minutes.
⚡ Key Lesson: When a shop has flammable substances, a fire will burn fast. Time for people to get out safely could be very short.
Classifications

Classes of Hazardous Substances

They are grouped into classes because, within the class, the chemical properties and risks are similar.

The categories/classes are:

  • EXPLOSIVE — can blow up / explode (e.g., fireworks)
  • FLAMMABLE — can catch fire
  • OXIDISING — break down sometimes just with heat applied, which leads to release of oxygen to fuel a fire
  • CORROSIVE — burns skin and metal and will damage eyes
  • TOXIC — poisonous — probably used to kill bugs and other things
  • ECOTOXIC — harms the environment

You will learn about the classes and chemical properties soon.

Our Products

Our Two Main Types of Hazardous Substances

Class 2 — Flammable Aerosols
Class 3 — Flammable Liquids
Class 2 — Flammable Aerosols
  • Pressurised containers
  • Contain flammable propellant gas
  • May explode if heated or punctured
  • Vapours can catch fire
Class 3 — Flammable Liquids
  • Liquid fuels, cleaners and solvents
  • Release flammable vapours that can catch fire
  • Liquid itself also can catch fire

You can recognise these substances mainly by the hazard symbols on their containers.

Storage Rules

Incompatible Substances

Incompatible substances must not be stored or mixed because they may react adversely together.

The SDS will provide specific information.

Our Rules
  • If the classes of the products are different, they must be treated as incompatible
  • Aerosols are also incompatible with other flammable gases (e.g., butane)
  • Can you spot the different, and incompatible, classes stored together in the pictures?
What About Our Aerosol Paints?
  • They are classified as flammable aerosols (class 2.1.2A).
  • They cannot therefore be stored with paints in paint containers or methylated spirits (class 3 or flammable liquids).
  • Different classes means they are incompatible and can't be placed together on the shelf.
Risk Management

Risk Depends on Type of Exposure

  • Risk is lower when containers are sealed and stored correctly
  • Risk is higher when containers are opened, mixed, or spilled

Keep this in mind during this course. For example, paint tinting has more risk because flammable vapours will be present.

⚠ Special Risk Question
Are there additional risks for a pregnant person (let's call her Jacinda) working with ADOS Plastic Putty? How would you find out?

You will learn how soon.
Case Study

Case Study: Jamey Bowring's Death was Caused by Ignition of Flammable Vapour

📋 Summary
  • Jamey Bowring was welding on top of a storage tank at Salter's Cartage yard in south Auckland in 2015.
  • Inside was both petroleum as a liquid and a large concentration of vapour.
  • Some of the petrol and other similar products turned into flammable vapour inside the tank.
  • Welding created the spark to ignite the vapour.
  • The tank exploded.
  • Jamey was thrown approximately 130 metres by the explosion and died.
  • The owner of Salters was prosecuted for his failures.
⚡ Key takeaway: Obvious risk, obvious poor work practice. Remember the risks coming from flammable vapour from flammable liquids.
Hazard Zones

Example of a Hazardous Area – AS 1940 Cabinet

These are the area around some hazardous substances where vapour may be in quantities that they could be flammable.

  • At Salters Cartage, this was a large area above and around the tank.
  • We use AS 1940 cabinets to store some flammable liquids.
  • This is a diagram of the hazardous area around an AS 1940 cabinet.
  • Also shown is how this is drawn on a special site plan.
Gold Star Question
Did you guess already that sources of ignition must not be inside the hazardous area? If you did, a gold star!!
Health & Safety

How Chemicals Enter Your Body

🫁 Inhalation — Breathing in
  • Aerosol mist, vapours, dusts
  • Prevent: work in ventilated areas, never spray aerosols toward your face
🤢 Ingestion — Swallowing
  • Eating or smoking after handling chemicals without washing hands
  • Prevent: always wash hands before eating or drinking after being in contact with substances
🖐 Absorption — Skin and Eyes
  • Chemicals passing through skin into blood
  • Prevent: wear gloves and eye protection when handling chemicals
Risk Assessment

Risk Ladder — How Exposure Changes Risk

Risk increases when exposure increases.

LOW RISK
Sealed containers on shelves No vapours released · No skin contact
Example: Sealed engine oil or sealed aerosol on display
MEDIUM RISK
Controlled opening for work tasks Small transfer or mixing
Example: Paint mixing or tinting in a designated area
HIGH RISK
Uncontrolled release or spill Vapours filling the area · Direct skin contact
Example: Leaking aerosol or paint spill during mixing
Knowledge Check — Part 1 of 2

Questions 1 – 5

Answer each question before clicking to reveal the answer
❓ Question 1 — True / FalseA customer is an example of an "other person" that we owe duties to under HSWA.
TTrue
FFalse
✅ TRUE — HSWA requires workers and businesses to protect not just fellow workers but also "other persons" — and customers visiting the store are a clear example. This is why safe storage, correct labelling, and being able to answer customer questions about hazardous products all matter.
❌ The answer is TRUE — HSWA requires workers and businesses to protect not just fellow workers but also "other persons" — and customers visiting the store are a clear example.
❓ Question 2 — True / FalseMost of the hazardous substances sold at Repco are flammable — meaning they can catch fire.
TTrue
FFalse
✅ TRUE — the training tells us that most of our hazardous substances are flammable, which is why fire safety is so important in our stores.
❌ The answer is TRUE — most of our hazardous substances are flammable, which is why fire safety is so important in our stores.
❓ Question 3 — True / FalseThe only risk that users of aerosols should be concerned about is that the containers may explode in a fire.
TTrue
FFalse
✅ FALSE — aerosols have multiple risks. As well as the risk of explosion when heated or punctured, the flammable propellant vapours can catch fire, and many aerosols also have health hazards — for example, repeated inhalation of some aerosol vapours can cause long-term health effects such as cancer. Always check the SDS and the pictograms on the label.
❌ The answer is FALSE — aerosols have multiple risks including explosion, flammable vapours catching fire, and long-term health hazards such as cancer from repeated inhalation.
❓ Question 4 — Multiple ChoiceWhich of these is true when it comes to opening paint for mixing or tinting purposes?
AThere is a risk that some of the paint may vaporise
BThere must not be sources of ignition nearby
CThis activity is higher risk generally because the containers are opened
DAll of the above
✅ D — All of the above are true. Opening paint for mixing or tinting releases flammable vapours, which means ignition sources must be kept away, and the activity is higher risk than simply having sealed containers on the shelf.
❌ The answer is D — All of the above are true. Opening paint for mixing or tinting releases flammable vapours, requires no ignition sources nearby, and is higher risk than sealed containers on the shelf.
❓ Question 5 — True / FalseVapour that comes from flammable liquids is unlikely to be flammable.
TTrue
FFalse
✅ FALSE — vapour from flammable liquids IS flammable and can be very dangerous. This is exactly what killed Jamey Bowring — petroleum vapour inside a tank ignited when a welding spark was created. Vapour can be invisible and travel some distance from the liquid source.
❌ The answer is FALSE — vapour from flammable liquids IS flammable and very dangerous. Petroleum vapour killed Jamey Bowring when a welding spark ignited it. Vapour can be invisible and travel some distance from the liquid source.
Legal Duties

Worker Duties Under HSWA — Section 45

You (Workers) must:

  • Take reasonable care of your own health and safety
  • Make sure your actions do not harm others
  • Follow reasonable instructions
  • Follow our health and safety procedures

You have these legal duties.

Coming Up
You are about to learn about an example of an obvious breach by a worker of his section 45 duty.
Case Study — Section 45 Breach

Auckland Wharf Explosion (2022)

📋 Auckland Construction Site, 2022

At a construction site in Auckland in 2022, a shipping container had an LPG barbecue cylinder inside where the workers cooked their breakfast.

  • The LPG cylinder was leaking.
  • At 6:20am, four workers opened the doors to the container.
  • One worker (Joe) smelt LPG and said, "I wonder if I can light this." He used his lighter.
  • The LPG vapour ignited and caused an explosion.
  • Four workers were seriously injured.
  • In 2026, Joe would be prosecuted under Section 45.
⚡ The smell was a warning. Lighting the vapour was reckless. Serious harm resulted.
Hazardous Substance Identification

What Was the Hazardous Substance that Caused the Wynyard Explosion?

  • Propane and butane are the gases that are compressed into LPG cylinders
  • These gases are highly flammable and explosive
  • LPG is required to have an odorant (to give it the smell that you probably recognize) so leaks can be detected
  • The smell was a warning
  • Be aware of the risks
  • Be careful
Classification Systems

NZ Uses Two Hazard Classification Systems — HSNO and GHS

New Zealand uses both the HSNO classifications and Globally Harmonised System (GHS) to describe the classes. You may still see both systems on products and in SDS documents.

HSNO uses numbers and letters whereas GHS uses words alongside:

HSNO Codes
  • 2.1.1A — Flammable Gas (Category A) such as LPG
  • 2.1.2A — Flammable Aerosol (Category A)
  • 3.1C — Flammable Liquid (Category C)
  • 6.1E — Toxic (Category E)
  • 8.3A — Corrosive (Category A)
GHS Examples
  • Flammable Liquid — Category 1 (e.g., petrol)
  • Flammable Liquid — Category 3 (e.g., most paints)
  • Acute Toxicity (Oral) — Category 4 (e.g., pesticides such as Spider Spray)
  • Skin Corrosion — Category 1 (e.g., sulphuric acid)
Classification

How to Read a HSNO Code

Example: A Class 3.1 product like paint.

Category C — What It Means
Category C means the product has a higher flash point (it vapourises less easily) compared to Category A products like petrol.
Letter = Degree of Hazard
  • A = Highest danger
  • B = High danger
  • C = Medium danger
  • E = Lowest regulated level

So 3.1A would be more dangerous than 3.1C.

Classification

GHS (Globally Harmonised System)

GHS uses hazard classes and numbered categories. Having the words (GHS) actually makes it easier to link the risk and the classification.

Flammable Liquid — Category 1
Highest risk — e.g., petrol. Extremely flammable.
Flammable Liquid — Category 3
e.g., most paints sold at Repco.
Acute Toxicity (Oral) — Category 4
e.g., pesticides such as Spider Spray. Harmful if swallowed.
Skin Corrosion — Category 1
e.g., sulphuric acid. Causes severe burns.
Labels & Symbols

How to Read a GHS Classification

In Store
Methylated spirits is typically Category 2. Diesel is usually Category 3 or 4.
Case Links
Joe (at Wynyard) lit LPG — a flammable gas, category 1 (HSNO 2.1.1A).

Jamie was killed by an explosion involving petroleum products — flammable liquid, category 2 (HSNO class 3.1B).
Health & Safety

Health Hazards — What They Mean for You

Key Message
Some hazardous products cause immediate harm.

Others cause long-term health effects after repeated exposure.

Always check:

  • Signal word (Danger / Warning)
  • Pictograms
  • SDS Section 2 and Section 11

Keep it tight.

Health & Safety

Health Hazards in Practice

Most hazardous substances have adverse health effects for several reasons.

An aerosol could be both flammable and could cause cancer over time (if the vapour is inhaled regularly).

⚠ ADOS Plastic Putty
This product is flammable, and it could cause cancer over time.

Can you guess yet about the risks for Jacinda from using this product when pregnant?
Using the SDS

Investigating a Product Further

Question
"Is ADOS Plastic Putty safe for our pregnant customer, Jacinda, to use?"
Answer — Check the SDS
"The SDS will describe the hazards of the product". You will learn more about these in Part B of this course.
You Checked the SDS and Found This
The SDS hazard statements (shown left) identify any risks to reproductive health or an unborn child.
Essential Tool

Safety Data Sheets (SDS)

In the next module, we will work through some of the sections of an SDS.

The key SDS sections you need:

SectionContent
1Product Identification
2Classification (e.g. 3.1C Flammable Liquid)
3Ingredients / Composition
4First Aid Measures
5Fire Fighting Measures
6Accidental Release Measures (Spill Response)
7Safe Handling and Storage
8PPE / Exposure Controls
9Physical and Chemical Properties
10Incompatible substances — what NOT to store near it
11Toxicological Information
12Ecological Information
13Disposal
14Transport Information
15Regulatory Information
16Other Information
Accessing Information

How You Can Access SDS

⚠ Input from GPC Required
<input from GPC required>
  • Through the store system
  • From the supplier website
  • By asking your manager
  • By scanning QR codes (if available)
Legal Requirement
SDS must be available to workers.
Safe Procedures

Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)

An SOP is a written list of steps for safely carrying out a hazardous task.

We have SOP's for:

  • Receiving and unpacking aerosol deliveries
  • Cleaning up a small chemical spill
  • Disposing of expired aerosol cans
  • Using a fire extinguisher
Emergency Equipment

Spill Kit

A spill kit contains everything needed to safely clean up a hazardous spill.

Every Repco store must have a spill kit. Every team member must know where it is.

A spill kit contains:

  • Nitrile gloves
  • Safety goggles or face shield
  • Absorbent pads or granules
  • Sealable waste bags
  • Warning tape or cones
  • You might not know what the spill contains, so start by using the PPE to cover your eyes and hands.
⚠ Important
Never clean up a large spill without proper training and PPE. Evacuate the area and call for help.
Knowledge Check — Part 2 of 2

Questions 6 – 10

Answer each question before clicking to reveal the answer
❓ Question 6 — Multiple ChoiceSomeone who works with methylated spirits could suffer harm through...
ASplashing it into their eyes
BBreathing in the vapours
CGetting it on their skin repeatedly
DAll of the above
✅ D — All of the above. Methylated spirits can harm you through absorption (eyes and skin contact), inhalation (breathing in vapours), and repeated skin exposure. Always wear appropriate PPE — gloves, eye protection, and work in a ventilated area.
❌ The answer is D — All of the above. Methylated spirits can harm you through absorption (eyes and skin contact), inhalation (breathing in vapours), and repeated skin exposure. Always wear appropriate PPE.
❓ Question 7 — True / FalseUnder Section 45 of the Health and Safety at Work Act (HSWA), if you knowingly do something at work that creates danger and harm for a fellow worker, that is likely to be a breach of your legal duties.
TTrue
FFalse
✅ TRUE — Section 45 says you must take reasonable care of your own health and safety AND make sure your actions do not harm others. Joe at the Auckland wharf explosion was prosecuted under this section.
❌ The answer is TRUE — Section 45 says you must take reasonable care of your own health and safety AND make sure your actions do not harm others. Joe at the Auckland wharf explosion was prosecuted under this section.
❓ Question 8 — True / FalseIf two products have different HSNO classes, our standard rule is that they are INCOMPATIBLE and must not be stored together.
TTrue
FFalse
✅ TRUE — our rule is that if the classes of products are different, they must be treated as incompatible. For example, aerosol paints (Class 2) cannot be stored with flammable liquids like methylated spirits (Class 3).
❌ The answer is TRUE — if the classes of products are different, they must be treated as incompatible. Aerosol paints (Class 2) cannot be stored with flammable liquids like methylated spirits (Class 3).
❓ Question 9 — Multiple ChoiceA customer (let's call her Jacinda) is pregnant and very concerned about the risks of using ADOS Plastic Putty. Where is the BEST place to find information to help her?
AAsk a workmate what they think
BCheck the product label only
CCheck the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) — especially Section 2 (Hazard Statements)
DTell her the product is probably fine
✅ C — The SDS Section 2 contains the hazard statements and will give the specific information needed, including any risks for pregnant workers.
❌ The answer is C — The SDS Section 2 contains the hazard statements and will give the specific information needed, including any risks for pregnant workers.
❓ Question 10 — True / FalseA Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) is a written list of steps for safely carrying out a hazardous task, such as cleaning up a chemical spill.
TTrue
FFalse
✅ TRUE — SOPs give you clear, safe steps for hazardous tasks like receiving aerosol deliveries, cleaning up spills, and using fire extinguishers.
❌ The answer is TRUE — SOPs give you clear, safe steps for hazardous tasks like receiving aerosol deliveries, cleaning up spills, and using fire extinguishers.
Waste Management

Disposing of Hazardous Waste Safely

Hazardous waste is generated when products expire, become damaged, or are returned.

  • Never pour flammable liquids or solvents down the drain
  • Never crush or burn aerosol cans — they can explode
  • Always check Section 13 of the SDS for disposal instructions
  • Contact your manager before disposing of any damaged or expired product
  • Use only approved hazardous waste collection services
Personal Protection

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) — Your Last Line of Defence

PPE protects you when other controls cannot fully remove the risk.

PPE you may need at Repco:

  • Safety glasses or goggles — when handling aerosols and liquid chemicals
  • Chemical-resistant gloves — when handling flammable liquids and solvents
  • Respirator or dust mask — where inhalation is a risk
  • Protective apron — when risk of skin contamination
  • Face shield — when risk of chemical splash
✓ Check the SDS
Check the SDS to confirm which PPE is required for each product.
Knowledge Check

PPE — Test Your Understanding

❓ Knowledge CheckWhere would you find out which specific PPE is required when handling a particular product at Repco?
AAsk a colleague what they usually use
BCheck the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) — Section 8
CLook at the product advertisement
DUse whatever PPE is nearby
✅ Correct! Section 8 of the SDS specifies exactly which PPE is required for each product.
❌ Not quite. Always check SDS Section 8 (PPE / Exposure Controls) for the correct PPE for each product.
Module 1 — Complete

Module 1 Summary

What you have learned in Module 1:

✓HSWA 2015 and Regulation 4.5 set your legal obligations when handling hazardous substances
✓As a worker under Section 45, you must take reasonable care of your own health and safety and ensure your actions do not harm others
✓Most hazardous substances at Repco are flammable, creating a primary risk of fire and explosion
✓Our main classes are 2.1 (flammable aerosols) and 3.1 (flammable liquids)
✓Risk depends on exposure — how the product is stored, handled, opened, or spilled
✓Chemicals can enter the body through inhalation, ingestion, and skin or eye absorption
✓Hazardous substances may present physical, health, and environmental hazards
✓NZ uses both HSNO and GHS classification systems — you must understand how to read labels and pictograms
✓The Safety Data Sheet (SDS) provides essential information on classification, handling, PPE, incompatibilities, fire response, and disposal
✓Incompatible substances must never be stored or mixed together — always check Section 10 of the SDS
✓Control measures such as SOPs, spill kits, PPEs are in place to reduce risk
✓Ignoring hazards or introducing ignition sources can result in serious harm, prosecution, and fines

→ Proceed to Module 2 to learn about your specific products in detail.
